text:
GROUND TRUTH — tenex-edge (/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge), Rust, 38.5k LOC in src/, 374 passing `cargo test --lib` unit tests (verified: ran it, all green) + 49 integration tests behind local-relay/NIP-29-relay harnesses + 3 `--ignored` live-relay probes.

## 1. What it actually does (runtime flow)

Each AI coding-agent session (Claude Code, Codex, opencode, Grok CLI today) is wired via a host-neutral **hook**: on session start, the hook shells out to the `tenex-edge` binary, which talks to a single **per-machine daemon** over a Unix domain socket (spawning it if absent). The daemon:
- resolves/mints a durable **Nostr keypair** for `(agent-slug, machine)` — `src/identity.rs` — stored at `~/.tenex-edge/agents/<slug>.json` [...] Tagline material already in the crate description: *"Citizenship for your agents: durable identity + awareness on the Nostr fabric, host-neutral."* [...] **Commit to: THE FABRIC — a thin, always-on layer floating above every terminal, where agents exist as persistent beings while hosts remain disposable bodies.**

The doctrine's own line is the crown jewel and should appear verbatim in the copy: *"The host is a body; the identity is the person."* The world we're painting: your machine is full of brilliant workers sitting in adjacent rooms who, today, have no idea the others exist — and who are euthanized and reborn as amnesiacs every session. tenex-edge doesn't manage them, hire them, or conduct them. It gives them names that stick and a room tone: presence, a live activity line, and the ability to lean over and say something to a colleague.
